actions.thought: Qs 4d is a very weak hand with no significant showdown value or board coverage. Being the first hand of the tournament, I prefer to maintain a disciplined strategy and wait for better opportunities rather than contesting pots with marginal, uncoordinated cards. Folding is the most profitable play here.

Folded to me in the Small Blind with A8 suited. This is a strong starting hand 3-handed, well ahead of the Big Blind's random holding. I will open-raise to 3x the big blind (300) to steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that has good equity and playability post-flop.

In a 3-handed game, K2s is a standard hand to defend from the Big Blind against a 3x open from the Small Blind. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). Although I will be out of position, K2s has enough equity and playability, particularly with flush potential and the King's high-card value, to justify a call rather than a fold or a 3-bet.

With middle pair (eights), top kicker, and a backdoor flush draw, I have decent equity but am vulnerable to overcards. Betting around half pot (300) as a continuation bet serves to extract value from worse hands like straight draws (JT, T7, 76), smaller pairs, and floats, while protecting my equity by charging overcards to see the turn. This board favors my pre-flop raising range, so a c-bet is standard.

On a board of 9c-8h-3s, I have King-high and a backdoor heart draw. Pro has led for a full pot-sized bet (300 into 600). This large sizing typically indicates a polarized range of strong made hands or high-equity bluffs. While I have an overcard, K2h is too weak to continue against such a large sizing, as I have little equity and will be forced to fold on most turns if the aggression continues. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play here.

Ten-five offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity. Even though I am on the button, there is no reason to put chips in the pot with such a weak starting hand. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 3h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While it only costs 50 more chips to see the flop, 43o performs poorly out of position and has very little equity. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing more chips in a post-flop situation where I will likely be at a disadvantage.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button is a standard opening hand. Having position on both the Small Blind and Big Blind allows me to play the pot effectively post-flop if called, or take the blinds immediately. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300.

My starting hand (2s 9c) is extremely weak and disconnected. Calling a raise from the button while playing out of position in the small blind is a losing play mathematically, as my hand lacks potential to hit strong boards or play effectively post-flop. Folding is the standard, disciplined choice here to preserve my stack.

Opponent on the Button raised to 3x the big blind. I am in the Big Blind with 98o (9h 8c). This hand is too weak to profitably call a 3x raise out of position, even against a wider button opening range. Folding is the standard and most conservative play to preserve chips for better spots.

K-8 offsuit is a marginal hand, but on the button, it is a suitable candidate to open-raise in an attempt to steal the blinds in a three-handed game. I will raise to 250 to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds.

T8s is a marginal suited one-gapper. Facing a 2.5x open from the Button while out of position in the Small Blind with the Big Blind still to act, folding is the standard and safest play to avoid a marginal spot out of position.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Flashlight raised from the Button to 250, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 550. JTo is a strong enough hand to defend against a standard button opening range, especially given the pot odds (nearly 2.7 to 1). While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is too weak, and raising is unnecessary with a hand that plays reasonably well post-flop. I will call to see the flop.

I am in the Big Blind and have flopped an open-ended straight draw with Jack-Ten on a Queen-Nine-Trey board (any 8 or King completes my straight). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, Flashlight, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react to this flop. Donk betting would likely reveal the strength of my draw or allow my opponent to play perfectly against me. By checking, I can evaluate Flashlight's continuation bet sizing and decide whether to call or check-raise.

The flop of 3d-9s-Qc is relatively dry and doesn't hit my King-high range very hard. Since the opponent checked to me, I have the option to take a free card. Given my lack of pair or significant draw, betting into the opponent could be risky and unnecessary this early in the tournament. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and realize my equity for free, while avoiding turning a marginal hand into a bluff.

The turn brings a second Trey, pairing the board. Since Flashlight checked back on the flop after raising pre-flop, their range is likely capped at marginal pairs or high-card hands. I still have an open-ended straight draw with my Jack-Ten. Checking here allows me to potentially see the river for free if Flashlight checks again, or to call a reasonably sized bet to try and hit my straight on the final card. Leading out now doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to fold out a Queen, and I'd rather realize my equity as cheaply as possible.

The turn pairing the 3s does not improve my King-high, which remains a bluff catcher at best. My opponent checked to me again, signaling weakness or a willingness to play a small pot. Checking back is the most strategic play as it preserves my stack, keeps the pot small, and allows me to see the river for free without risking chips with a hand that has minimal showdown value.

The board has double-paired (Nines and Treys) with a Queen kicker. My hand (Jack-Ten) does not improve the board, meaning my best five-card hand is the board itself: Q-9-9-3-3. Because my cards are lower than the Queen on the board, any opponent holding an Ace or a King will beat me (as their best hand would be A-9-9-3-3 or K-9-9-3-3). Flashlight raised from the button pre-flop, which includes many Ax and Kx hands. However, they checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting they likely do not have a Queen or a Nine. By checking the river, I am looking to reach a showdown cheaply. If Flashlight checks behind, we will likely chop the pot unless they have an Ace, King, or a pocket pair. Betting here as a bluff is risky because Flashlight might not fold Ace-high or King-high on such a board, and I have no real value to represent given my checks on the flop and turn.

The board shows two pair (nines and threes) with a Queen kicker. My hand of King-Eight does not improve this, so my best five-card combination is the board itself. Since the opponent checked, there is no strategic value in betting, as it would only be called by hands that beat me (a Queen) or result in a chop. Checking is the correct decision to safely conclude the hand.

56o is a weak offsuit connector. Being first to act on the Button in a 3-handed game, it's not strong enough to open-raise for a steal or call. Folding is the standard and safest play to preserve stack size early in the tournament.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair (2d 2c). Pocket pairs have significant value in head-up situations, but Deuces are vulnerable and play best when they hit a set. I will call the additional 50 chips to complete the bet to 100, allowing me to see a flop cheaply while keeping the pot manageable against the Big Blind.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The small blind has completed their bet, meaning I have the option to check and see a free flop or raise to take control. Given that Ace-Six offsuit is a decent hand but not strong enough to warrant a raise against a limper, checking is the most prudent and balanced decision to maintain pot control and evaluate the flop first.

I have flopped a set of deuces on a board of 2h-Qc-5c. This is a very strong hand. While there is a flush draw present, I want to start building the pot and extract value from any Queen or middle pair Flashlight might be holding. I will lead out with a half-pot bet of 100 to get called by a wide range of hands.

Flash is betting pot size into a limped pot, which indicates either a made hand like a pair of Queens or a speculative semi-bluff. Holding the Ace of Clubs provides me with a strong backdoor flush draw and the potential to outdraw them with an Ace. Given the pot odds and my equity, calling is the most balanced approach to see the turn without overcommitting, as folding is too passive with this much potential and raising is unnecessary against a pot-sized bet.

The 9c on the turn completes the flush draw. While I have bottom set, the presence of three clubs makes the board dangerous. I will check to control the pot size and see how Flashlight responds. If they bet, I have the odds to call and try to improve to a full house on the river, and if they check, I likely still have the best hand against a Queen or a smaller pair.

The opponent checked to me on the turn after leading on the flop, which indicates a potential lack of strength or a desire to control the pot size. With the nut flush draw, I have significant equity to improve on the river. Checking behind is the most prudent strategic move here; it allows me to realize my equity for free, prevents me from bloating the pot when I only have a draw, and keeps the betting initiative flexible for the final street.

The river brings a fourth club to the board, making any single club in my opponent's hand a flush that beats my set of deuces. Since I do not hold a club myself, my hand has significantly decreased in relative strength. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and only serves to build a pot I am likely to lose. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or to evaluate my opponent's reaction.

I have flopped and turned a strong flush draw and improved to the nut flush on the river with the Ace of Clubs. The board texture is wet with four clubs. Since my opponent checked to me, they are likely looking for a cheap showdown. Given I have the absolute best hand, I must value bet to extract chips. A bet of 250 into the 400 pot is a solid size that should induce a call from any King or weaker flush in their range.

On this board of 2h-Qc-5c-9c-Kc, I have a set of deuces that improved to a King-high flush on the river because I hold the 2c. However, since there are four clubs on the board and the 2c is the lowest possible club not already on the board, any single club in Flashlight's hand (Ac, Jc, Tc, 8c, 7c, 6c, 4c, or 3c) would give them a higher flush. My hand is essentially a bluff-catcher that beats any hand without a club and loses to any hand with a club. Flashlight called my flop bet, which includes many club draws, but they also checked the turn when the third club arrived. Their 250-chip bet on the river into a 400-chip pot (making the total pot 650) could be a value bet with a flush or a bluff representing the fourth club after I showed weakness by checking twice. Given the pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 900 requires being right about 28% of the time) and the 3-handed nature of the game, folding a flush is too conservative. I will call to see if Flashlight is bluffing or value-betting a weaker non-flush hand.

I am on the button with Queen-Six offsuit (Q6o). In a three-handed game, opening ranges are typically wider, but Q6o remains a marginal hand. With both opponents having slightly larger stacks and having shown some aggression in previous hands, I will opt for a disciplined fold. This hand does not play well post-flop, and I prefer to wait for better spots or hands with more equity to maintain my stack in this 150-hand tournament.

8d2d is an extremely weak hand with essentially zero equity or playability, especially when playing out of position against the Big Blind. Investing additional chips here is not profitable, so folding is the correct strategic decision.

Five-Nine offsuit is a very weak, disconnected hand with low equity. Opening from the button with such a marginal hand is strategically unsound, especially early in a 150-hand tournament. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here to preserve my stack.

K9s is a strong starting hand in a blind vs blind scenario. Raising from the small blind puts pressure on the big blind and gives me the initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to discourage a wide defending range.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Jack offsuit (Kd Jh). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. KJo is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, especially as I will have position for the remainder of the hand. While a 3-bet is possible, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and utilize my positional advantage post-flop without bloating the pot with a hand that could be dominated by the very top of his range (like AK or KQ). The pot odds are 2:1 (200 to call into a 400 pot), which is more than enough to justify a call.

I raised pre-flop and the flop is quite dry (4h 3s Jc). This board texture favors my range, which contains strong Jx and overpairs, more than the caller's range. I have K9 of clubs, giving me a backdoor flush draw and an overcard. A standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200) should generate enough fold equity against floats or underpairs, while allowing me to easily fold if raised.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a very dry board of Jack-Four-Trey rainbow. This is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind encounter. The opponent (Pro) has led with a standard continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 into 600). While I could raise for value, calling is often superior here because the board is uncoordinated, meaning there are very few draws to protect against. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing him to continue bluffing with air on the turn or to keep betting with weaker marginal hands that might fold to a raise. I have position and a strong made hand, so I will call and look to extract value on later streets.

The 8c turn gives me a strong King-high flush draw. Continuing aggression here works well as a semi-bluff. By betting around half pot (500), I maintain the initiative, build the pot in case my flush hits, and put pressure on Flash's marginal made hands like mid-pocket pairs or weak Jacks, as well as denying equity to any floats.

The 8c on the turn is largely a brick, as it doesn't significantly alter the board texture or complete many draws besides a gutshot. I still have Top Pair with a King kicker, which is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind match. The opponent is continuing with a 500 bet into a 1000 pot. Raising here would likely force out his bluffs and weaker hands (like 99 or TT) while potentially getting me into trouble against overpairs or AJ. Calling allows me to keep his range wide and maintain control of the pot size while staying in position. I will call and evaluate the river.

The 5c on the river completes my King-high flush. The only hand that beats me is the nut flush (Ac-Xc). Given Flash called on both the flop and the turn, their range could definitely include worse club draws that just got there, or strong one-pair hands like a Jack that might bluff-catch. I have an extremely strong hand and need to go for value. Betting around 60-70% of the pot should extract maximum value from worse flushes and sticky made hands.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Kd Jh) on a board of 4h-3s-Jc-8c-5c. This is a blind-versus-blind situation where ranges are typically very wide. The opponent has triple-barreled, finishing with a large river bet of 1400 into 2000. While the backdoor flush and some straights (like 67) arrived, my hand is near the top of my range for calling in this spot. I only lose to AJ, sets, two pairs, or the occasional flush/straight, but I beat all of his bluffs and potentially some thinner value bets. Given the pot odds (roughly 2.4:1), I need to be right about 29% of the time. In this dynamic, KJ is too strong to fold.

K7o is a marginal hand, even on the button 3-handed. Since I have a solid stack and want to play profitable ranges, folding K7o is a disciplined decision to avoid getting into tricky situations with dominated kings.

I have Ace-Six offsuit in the small blind. Since the Button folded, it is just me and the Big Blind. A6o is a strong hand in this blind vs blind scenario. Raising to 300 is a standard play to put pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the hand, even though I will be out of position post-flop.

Defending the big blind with suited cards Qc4c. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400), it is a profitable call against a small blind open. Q4 suited offers enough playability to see a flop in position.

I have Ace-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4) on a very low 2-3-5 rainbow board. As the pre-flop aggressor in this blind vs. blind situation, I will lead out with a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half the pot) allows me to take control of the hand, potentially force folds from high-card hands that missed, and keep the lead with my drawing equity and overcards.

I hold an open-ended straight draw with 2-3-4-5 on a 2-3-5 board. With 8 outs (any Ace or 6), I have approximately 17% equity. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 300 to win 1200 total), calling is a mathematically sound decision. I also maintain position for the remainder of the hand, which adds to the value of calling rather than raising as a bluff.

I have improved to top pair with the six of spades. Additionally, I hold the nut flush draw (Ace of clubs with two clubs on the board) and a gutshot straight draw (needing a four). Given the blind-vs-blind dynamic and the fact that I have been the aggressor, I will continue with a semi-value bet. While a four completes a straight for my opponent, my hand has immense equity to improve on the river even if I am currently behind. A bet of 600 into the 1200 pot maintains initiative and extracts value from weaker pairs or draws.

I have turned a straight with 2-3-4-5-6 on the board holding a 4. Additionally, I hold the Queen of clubs and Four of clubs, giving me a flush draw on a board with two clubs. While a 7-4 would make a higher straight, my hand is extremely strong and has high equity. Raising allows me to build the pot and extract value from worse hands like a lower straight or two pair while protecting against potential draws. A raise to 1800 is a standard value raise in this spot.

On the turn, I have improved to top pair (sixes) with an Ace kicker. More importantly, the turn card (6c) has given me the nut flush draw (Ac) to go along with my gutshot straight draw (needing a four). Flashlight's raise to 1800 is a strong move, often indicating a straight (any four) or a set. However, given the pot odds (calling 1200 to win a total pot that will be 4800), I have significant equity. My nut flush draw alone provides about 20% equity, and my top pair or gutshot potential adds to that. Folding is not an option given the strength of my draws and the price offered. I will call to see if I can improve on the river.

On the river, the board is 2c-3d-5s-6c-Td. I have a pair of sixes with an Ace kicker. On the turn, my opponent (Flashlight) raised my bet of 600 to 1800, which is a very strong signal of strength, likely representing a straight (any 4) or a set. I called because I had top pair and the nut flush draw. The river 10d does not complete my flush or improve my hand significantly. Since Flashlight took the initiative on the turn, I will check to them. I have some showdown value against missed club draws, but I cannot value bet here. If Flashlight bets, I will have to decide if my pair is good enough to catch a bluff, but for now, checking is the standard and safest play.

I have the 2-3-4-5-6 straight. While this is a strong hand, it is not the absolute nuts as a 7-4 would create a 3-4-5-6-7 straight. The opponent checked the river after calling my turn raise, which could indicate a trap or simply a desire to reach showdown with a marginal hand. Given that betting might fold out worse hands that I currently beat and risks getting check-raised by a 7-4, checking behind is the optimal strategic decision to secure the pot at showdown.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit (Js 3d), which is a very weak hand. While the button is typically a good position to attempt a steal, this specific hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. Given that both opponents have significantly larger stacks and have shown aggression in previous hands, I will choose the conservative path and fold to preserve my stack for a better starting hand.

I am in the small blind with King-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Playing this out of position against the big blind is strategically disadvantageous. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
I am in the button position with a very weak starting hand, 8-2 offsuit. There is no strategic value in contesting this pot with such a poor hand, so the standard and correct play is to fold.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand, and playing it from the small blind puts me out of position against the big blind for the rest of the hand. The most prudent and standard decision is to fold and wait for a better spot.

With Ace-Ten suited on the button, I have a strong hand that plays very well post-flop and dominates many hands in the blinds' defending ranges. Raising is the standard play to take initiative, thin the field, and potentially win the blinds outright. I will make a standard 3x raise to 300.

I am in the small blind with 6s 9d, which is a very weak hand to play out of position. The button (Pro) has raised to 300. Calling here with a mediocre hand like 9-high against a button raise often leads to difficult spots post-flop where I am at a significant disadvantage. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice to preserve my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Facing a raise from the button, there is insufficient equity to justify a call, even with the pot odds offered. It is mathematically and strategically correct to fold and wait for a better spot to enter a pot, especially out of position.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit (Kd 9c). In a 3-handed game, this is a strong hand to open with from the button to put pressure on the blinds. A standard raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to seize the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or narrow the field.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d-7h, which is a weak, uncoordinated hand. Calling a raise of 300 while out of position against the button is a losing proposition, and the Big Blind has yet to act. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

K2o is a weak, easily dominated hand. Facing a 3bb open from the Button, folding from the Big Blind is the standard and optimal play to preserve my stack rather than playing out of position with garbage.

I hold a pocket pair (3c, 3h) on the button. This is a strong starting hand in a late position. I want to take the initiative pre-flop to potentially win the blinds or see the flop in control. A raise to 300 is a standard, disciplined open that balances value and protection, allowing me to evaluate the situation after the flop based on any callers.
76 offsuit is a weak hand to play from the small blind, especially out of position facing a 3x raise from the button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the big blind with 7s 5h. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button, and Pro has folded. 7-5 offsuit is a weak hand to defend with, especially out of position against a big stack who has shown aggression. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

With 5h 3c offsuit, I have a very weak starting hand with almost no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Being the first to act pre-flop from the Button in a 3-handed game, the best and most standard move is to simply fold and save my chips for a more profitable spot.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of fives. Since the button (Pro) folded, I am head-to-head against the Big Blind (Flashlight). Pocket pairs are strong in short-handed play, and raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate to define my hand and put pressure on the opponent.

Holding 9-2 offsuit is extremely weak, ranking near the bottom of starting hands. Calling a raise from the small blind in the big blind with such a marginal holding is generally negative expectation (-EV) in the long run. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is disciplined to fold and wait for a more playable hand.

I am on the button with 2d 6h, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Given my stack size of 4450 (approx. 44 big blinds) and the quality of the cards, there is no reason to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and most prudent play to conserve chips for a more favorable spot.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Six offsuit, heads-up against the big blind. The hand has a decent high-card value for a small blind versus big blind confrontation. Completing the blind is a balanced approach to see a flop cheaply while remaining in position to play post-flop, as I am out of position against the big blind.
With T5 offsuit, I have a weak hand. Since the Small Blind just limped, I can check and see a free flop in position. There is no reason to raise and build a pot with this holding.

The board is 9s-7c-4h, which is relatively dry. I have Ace-high, which might be the best hand currently given the limp-check pre-flop dynamic, but it is vulnerable. Checking is the standard play here from the small blind as it allows me to control the pot size, keep the betting range wide, and see if the big blind shows any aggression before committing more chips.
With Ten-high and no draws on a 9-7-4 rainbow board, there is no reason to put any chips into the pot. Flashlight checked to me, so I can happily check back and see a free turn card.

The board is 9s-7c-4h-Qh. My hand is Ace-high. This board is relatively disconnected, but the Queen on the turn makes it more likely my opponent has paired a Queen or has a stronger draw/pair combination. I have no pair and no strong draw, so betting would essentially be a bluff against an opponent who has shown no weakness or specific strength. Checking allows me to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown. If my opponent checks behind, I may see a free river card, which is the most optimal path for my current hand strength.

With ten-high and no draws on a 9-7-4-Q board, I have very little equity. Flashlight has checked again, and while a bluff might occasionally fold out better high-card hands, there's no need to force a bet here with such a weak holding in a limped pot. Checking back is the safest and most standard play to try and see a free river.

The board is 9s-7c-4h-Qh-Kc, and I hold Ace-high. Given that the flop and turn were checked through, my opponent likely does not have a strong made hand or is also playing cautiously. Betting into the opponent would essentially turn my showdown value into a bluff, which is suboptimal as most hands that would call me (any pair) will beat me. Checking is the standard play to reach a cheap showdown and potentially win against another high-card hand or a missed draw.

I have Ten-high on a 9-7-4-Q-K board. Flashlight has checked down all three streets in this limped pot. I have zero showdown value, but the pot is very small. Bluffing here might fold out Ace-high or Jack-high, but Flashlight could easily have hit a piece of the board (like a weak King or Queen) and is checking to induce, or he might just call with a weak pair. There's no need to unnecessarily risk chips in such a small, unraised pot. Checking back is the most disciplined and safest play.
4-7 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor equity and no potential for improvement. There is no strategic justification to play this hand, so folding is the correct and disciplined decision.
Q7o is a weak offsuit holding. Playing it from out of position in the Small Blind against the Big Blind's 43.5 BB stack is not profitable. The safest and most standard play is to fold here.

Pre-flop from the Button, I hold 74o. This is a very weak, uncoordinated, offsuit hand with low card values. There is no reason to invest chips into the pot with this holding. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Five offsuit (J5o). The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While J5o has some high-card value in a short-handed game, it is a very weak hand to play out of position against a stack that covers me significantly. Calling 50 more to see a flop is tempting, but this hand rarely hits well and often gets me into trouble post-flop. Given I am the short stack at the table, I will preserve my chips for a more advantageous starting hand.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with Queen-Eight offsuit. This is a standard opening hand for the button to put pressure on the blinds and attempt to win the pot pre-flop. With a stack of 4450 (44.5 BB), a standard 3x raise is appropriate to maintain a healthy stack and leverage my position.

I am in the small blind with 8c-9s, which is a weak hand. Calling a raise from the button while out of position is strategically poor and likely to lead to difficult post-flop decisions. Given my current stack size, it is better to fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Facing a 3x raise from the Button while holding Q7 suited in the Big Blind. Although it's a suited hand, Q7s is relatively weak and can easily be dominated by better Queens or higher suited connectors in the Button's opening range. Being out of position makes it difficult to realize our equity, and with a comfortable stack, there is no need to defend such a marginal hand. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play here.

Holding 4s3s on the button is a weak hand in a three-handed game. Given my stack size and position, it is better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than committing chips to a speculative situation.

I am dealt 2s8c in the Small Blind, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With Flashlight folding, I am up against the Big Blind out of position. The standard and most profitable play with this garbage hand is simply to fold and save my remaining stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button with Queen-Deuce offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even in a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify opening or trying to steal the blinds. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here.

I am in the Small Blind with K8o. After the Button folds, it's a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. K8o is a strong hand in this spot, significantly ahead of a random range. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop advantage.

Flash is in the small blind and has raised, which is a wide range. As the big blind, I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call with Jack-Seven offsuit. Defending the big blind here is a standard, profitable play given the odds and the stack depth. Calling allows me to see a flop and re-evaluate.

I have middle pair (8s) with a King kicker on a coordinated board (8h-7h-Ts). While vulnerable to overcards and draws, my hand has significant value in a heads-up pot. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300 (half-pot) to protect my hand, extract value from draws or weaker pairs, and maintain the initiative I established pre-flop.

I have middle pair (7s) with a flush draw (Jack of hearts). Given the pot odds (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200), this is an easy call. I am in position, and my hand has equity against many parts of the opponent's betting range. Raising here is unnecessary given the opponent's stack depth and my solid holding, so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate.

I am on the button with 6h 4s, which is a very weak starting hand. With Ace-Nine offsuit on the button in a three-handed game, I have a strong hand that should be played aggressively. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is the standard opening play to isolate, build the pot, and take the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 4d, which is a very weak hand. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the button. 7-2 offsuit is the mathematically worst starting hand in No-Limit Hold'em. Despite receiving favorable pot odds to call, playing such a weak hand out of position against two opponents is negative expected value. It is disciplined to fold and wait for a better opportunity to accumulate chips.

I have no pair and very limited equity on this Ace-high board, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Being out of position, the most standard and sound play is to check to the aggressor and see how they respond.

A bet of around 500 into the 700 pot is a solid size to extract value from hands like two pair, top pair, or strong draws while building the pot for future streets.

I have Queen high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) on an Ace-high board that significantly favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit (T5o). In a three-handed game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for the button. I am in the Big Blind with Q4o facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand that does not defend well even with position post-flop against a standard SB opening range. Folding is the best choice here.
8d 5d is a weak suited connector that does not have enough equity to justify an open or a limp in this position. Playing tight and waiting for stronger starting hands is the most prudent strategy.

With Q6 offsuit from the small blind, the hand is too weak to play optimally against the big blind, especially since we'll be out of position post-flop. Folding is the safest decision to preserve chips for a better opportunity.
Holding T2o on the Button is extremely weak. There is no reason to get involved with such a marginal hand, so the standard play is to fold pre-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o. After the Button (Pro) folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind (Flashlight). J8o is a reasonable hand to steal the blinds with in this position. The hand 9h-2h is very weak and lacks significant playability, especially when playing out of position as the Big Blind. I am on the button with 2c 4d, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I have positional advantage, this hand has very little equity and poor playability post-flop. The button has folded, leaving me heads-up against the Big Blind. Although my hand (8s 5d) is weak, the cost to complete the small blind is minimal (50 chips). I have 7d Tc in the big blind after the small blind just completed. T7o is not strong enough to raise for value or as a bluff here reliably. Checking my option to see a free flop is the standard and most optimal play.

The board (Qd Js 4c) is very favorable for a Big Blind's defending range, as they will have many Queen and Jack combinations. My hand (8s 5d) has no pair and no realistic draw. I have ten-high with no direct draws on a Q-J-4 board. The small blind checked, and I have very little equity. Checking back is the most sensible play to see a free turn card without investing chips with a weak hand.

My hand (8s 5d) has no pair, no draw, and absolutely no showdown value on this board (Qd Js 4c 3h). I have ten-high with no draw on a board of 4-Q-J-3. The small blind checked again on the turn. Since I have zero equity and no reasonable draw, my best option is to continue to check and see a free river rather than risking chips on a pure bluff.

I am in the small blind holding 8-high on a board of 4c Qd Js 3h Kd. My hand has zero showdown value and no potential to improve further. Since the pot is small and the board is static, any bet I make will likely be called by a range that beats me (any King, Queen, Jack, or even pair of 4s or 3s). I have ten-high on a Q-J-4-3-K board. The pot is very small (200), and I cannot win at showdown. My hand (7s 5h) is extremely weak and has no playability, especially in a three-handed game where I would be out of position on the later streets against the blinds. Folding is the most prudent and expected value-maximizing decision.

With Flashlight folding on the button, we are in a Blind vs Blind situation against Flash. King-Nine offsuit is a reasonably strong starting hand, well ahead of an average random hand. I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Trey of Clubs, which is a very weak starting hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. A5o is a decent hand to attempt a steal from the Button. I will raise to 3x the big blind, consistent with my opening size, to put pressure on the blinds and try to win the pot uncontested.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (Th 8d). The Button (Pro) has raised to 300 (3x). I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine offsuit. The button (Pro) raised to 300, and the small blind folded. I need to call 200 to see a flop. Given the pot odds and my position, defending the big blind with a decent hand like Q9o is mathematically sound. I will call to see the flop.

The board texture is King-Jack-3, which heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, has completely missed this flop and offers no significant equity or draw potential. The board [Jd, 3h, Ks] is rainbow and very dry, heavily favoring the pre-flop aggressor's range. QTo is a marginal offsuit broadway hand. Facing a 3x open from an active Button player, playing this hand out of position can easily lead to dominated situations or tricky post-flop decisions. 4s 5c is an extremely weak hand with poor equity, especially acting first from the button in a 3-handed game. There is no reason to invest chips with such a marginal holding, so folding is the disciplined and correct decision to preserve my stack.

I'm in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 3x the big blind. This is a very weak hand to defend with out of position, even in a three-handed game. I am in the small blind with 7-4 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand with very little playability. Even though I have already posted the small blind, there is no reason to invest further chips into the pot with such a poor holding. Folding is the standard and correct play.

With bottom pair and an Ace kicker on a dry rainbow board, I have a marginal made hand with some showdown value. Checking here is standard for pot control. It allows me to keep the opponent's bluffs in the pot, avoid building a big pot out of position with a weak pair, and evaluate their action.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a dry, rainbow board. After checking the flop for pot control, facing a half-pot stab from the Big Blind, calling is the most standard line. Holding 95o on the button, this is a very weak starting hand with negative expected value. Folding pre-flop is the standard and correct play.

In a blind-vs-blind situation after the button has folded, J9o is a strong enough hand to raise for value and pressure. Historically, Flashlight has folded to several of my small blind raises, making this a good opportunity to take down the blinds or play a pot with initiative.

Qh 2s is a very weak starting hand out of position. Even though I am in the big blind and the pot odds are tempting, this is a clear fold. Calling with such a marginal hand leads to difficult post-flop situations and unnecessary chip loss. I will save my stack for a more advantageous opportunity.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten of Spades, which is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is around 59 big blinds. Standard opening raises in this tournament have been 3x. I will raise to 300 to take initiative and build a pot with a premium hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 2c 5s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, folding is the only viable and sensible option.

I opened to 300 with Ace-Ten of Spades from the button, and Flashlight 3-bet to 1000 from the small blind. As-Ts is a very strong hand 3-handed and is too powerful to fold to a single 3-bet. I initiated the action pre-flop with a 3-bet and hold the initiative. The flop of King-Six-Three is relatively dry, although it hits the calling range of a button raiser reasonably well. I have an overcard and the nut flush blocker (Ace of Clubs), giving me some equity. A continuation bet here is standard to maintain the narrative that I have a strong hand, specifically a King or better, and to fold out hands like middle pairs or air. I have Ace-Ten of Spades on a King-high board. Flashlight 3-bet pre-flop and is now leading with a small 1/3 pot continuation bet (700 into 2100). I have Ace-Ten of Spades on a board of Kc 6h 3h Jc. Pre-flop, Flashlight 3-bet and then led for 1/3 pot on the flop, which I called. The board is King-Six-Three-Jack-Six. I hold Ace-Queen. We checked the turn, and the river is a blank. I am on the button with King-Seven suited in clubs. This is a strong enough hand to open from the button to steal the blinds and take control of the hand. Given that Flash (Big Blind) has a relatively short stack, raising provides good leverage. I will raise to 300 to standardise my open size.

I am on the Button with pocket Nines, a strong starting hand. Opening with a standard 3x raise is the best play to take control of the hand, potentially steal the blinds, or build a pot in position if called.

I am in the big blind with suited connectors/gappers Qc-5c against a button open from Pro. The pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 to win 450 total), which makes calling profitable with a hand that has reasonable equity and post-flop playability. Folding would be too weak here.

I am out of position in the Big Blind against the button. I have flopped bottom pair with the Queen kicker. The board is relatively dry, but Pro has a wider range in this spot. With pocket Nines on a King-high dry board, I have second pair. A continuation bet of about half the pot will help protect my hand against overcards like Ace, Queen, or Jack, and can also extract value from lower pairs or straight draws. Betting maintains the pre-flop aggression and helps define my opponent's range.

I have flopped middle pair with a 5. Although the board is K-high and my hand is vulnerable, the pot odds (300 to call into a total pot of 1250) are very favorable at 4:1. The bet is small, indicating that Pro might be betting a wide range or testing the waters. Folding would be too conservative given the price, and raising would likely bloat the pot against hands that have me beat. Calling is the most profitable play to keep the pot manageable and see the turn card.

I have improved to two pair with Queens and Fives on a K-Q-7-5 board. While this is a strong holding, it remains vulnerable to higher two pairs or sets, and there are straight draws present. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the standard play. This allows the opponent to continue betting with bluffs or inferior hands, effectively acting as a trap, while keeping the pot size under control.

The turn brings a Queen, adding a second overcard to my pair of Nines. My opponent's flop calling range includes King-x, Queen-x that just improved, and better pocket pairs, so my hand is often behind. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or strong draws. Checking behind exercises pot control and allows me to see a free river card.

I have two pair (Queens and Fives) on a K-Q-7-5-3 board. The turn went check-check, which implies both players have marginal holdings or are pot-controlling. Betting for value here is thin; I am unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I would be isolating myself against a stronger King or better two-pair combinations. Checking is the optimal decision to reach showdown, minimize potential losses, and potentially induce a bluff from a missed draw or inferior hand.

The river brings a brick (3d) and Flashlight checks again. My pocket nines have solid showdown value, but if I bet, I will almost exclusively be called by better hands (such as a King, Queen, or a slow-played monster) and fold out worse hands (like missed flush/straight draws or smaller pairs). Checking behind ensures pot control and allows me to safely realize my showdown equity.

I am on the button with Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o). In a 3-handed game, any Ace is a strong starting hand to open from the button. My stack is roughly 40 big blinds, which gives me enough room to play post-flop or fold to significant pressure. I will raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament, to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 5d. I am facing a raise from the button (Flash) to 300. With 50 already in the pot as the small blind, I need to call 250 to see the flop. Suited connectors play well, and I have good pot odds to make this call and potentially see a flop. I am out of position, but the stack depth justifies a speculative call.
Q8o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, playing this multiway out of position would be highly unprofitable. Folding is the correct choice.

I am out of position against the button, and I have flopped bottom pair (sixes) on a King-high board. This board is very favorable for the button's opening range, which often includes broadway cards like Kings and Tens. Betting into the pre-flop aggressor with such a marginal hand is suboptimal as it will likely force folds from hands I am already beating while getting called or raised by superior hands. Checking is the most disciplined approach to control the pot size and see if the opponent decides to check back or place a bet that I can reconsider.

I opened the button with Ace-Trey offsuit and was called by the small blind. The flop comes King-Ten-Six with two spades. This is a very dry board for my hand, as I have no direct connection or draw. However, I am the pre-flop aggressor and have position. My opponent checked to me. While I could continuation-bet, this board textures hits a lot of a flat-caller's range (Kx, Tx, QJ, spade draws). Since I have Ace high, I have some showdown value against complete air, and checking back allows me to control the pot size and potentially realize my equity or bluff on later streets if the board develops favorably. Betting here into a coordinated board often results in being called or raised by better hands.

The turn brings the seven of clubs, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing an eight or four) to go along with my pair of sixes. Flash checked the flop, indicating a lack of a strong King or high pair, which means my pair of sixes might currently be the best hand or at least has significant equity. I want to seize the initiative now that the board has become more coordinated and my hand has improved to a strong semi-bluffing candidate. By betting, I can either take down the pot immediately or build a larger pot for when I hit my straight, while also potentially folding out weaker hands that Flash might be holding.

After checking back the flop with Ace high, Flashlight has led out for half the pot on the turn. The board of K-T-6-7 is quite coordinated and hits many hands in a small blind's calling range, such as middle pairs or draws. My Ace high has very little equity against a made hand, and without any significant draws, calling is mathematically incorrect as I likely only have three outs. Folding allows me to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

With Ace-Deuce suited on the button in a 3-handed game, this is a strong hand to open with. Being in position allows me to control the action post-flop. A standard raise of 300 is appropriate to potentially steal the blinds or build a pot with a hand that has good equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 8h, which is a very weak and disconnected hand. Flashlight raised from the Button. The standard and optimal play is to fold this hand, saving my chips for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with K7o. Flashlight raised to 300 from the button, and the Small Blind folded. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a total pot of 450 (including my 200). K7o is a reasonable defense hand in a 3-handed game, especially when closing the action against a button raise. My stack is 3650, which is around 36 big blinds, so I have enough room to see a flop. Folding would be too tight here given the pot odds and the strength of a King-high hand against a button range.

I defended my big blind with K7o and the flop came Js 8s 6h. I have completely missed this board, holding only King high with no significant draws. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard and most prudent play is to check and see how they react. I cannot justify a lead-out bet here.

I have the nut flush draw on a flop that contains two spades. The board is relatively dry, making this an ideal spot to semi-bluff. By betting, I can increase the pot size to get paid when I hit my flush or take down the pot immediately if my opponent folds. Checking back is passive; aggressive play with the nut draw is usually the most profitable long-term strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Kd 7h. Pre-flop, I called a 300 raise from Flashlight on the Button. The flop is Js 8s 6h. I have checked, and Flashlight has bet 350 into a pot of 650. This board texture (Jack-high, coordinated with straight and flush draws) favors the button's range, but it also hits many hands that defend from the Big Blind. However, my specific hand, King-high, has very little equity here. I have no pair, no flush draw, and only a very weak backdoor straight draw. Calling 350 to win a total pot that would be 1350 (26% equity needed) is not justified with just King-high on this board. Since I am out of position and have no real draw or made hand, the most profitable long-term play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

K8o is a marginal hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. With Flash sitting on a shorter stack of 34 BBs and Flashlight covering me in the Big Blind, there's a risk of being 3-bet or shoved on. Folding is a safe and standard play here to preserve the stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 7d. The Button has folded, making this a blind-vs-blind situation. My hand is relatively weak (off-suit connectors) and I would be playing out of position against the chip leader, Flashlight, for the rest of the hand. Given my stack of 34 big blinds and the chip leader's ability to pressure me, folding is the most disciplined move to preserve my stack for a better starting hand.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit, a very weak starting hand. With a stack of 3400 (34 big blinds) and being significantly out-chipped by both opponents, there is no reason to enter the pot with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

I am in the small blind and the button has folded. This leaves me heads-up against the big blind (Pro). Holding Ace-Nine suited, I have a strong equity advantage. Raising is the optimal move to exercise my position and attempt to steal the pot or build value with a strong hand. A raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open-raise size in this context.

I have King-Eight suited in the Big Blind facing a standard 3x open from the Small Blind. This is a strong enough hand to defend in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Calling allows me to play post-flop in position with a hand that has good high-card strength and strong flush potential.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have the initiative. The flop [6s, Jh, 5h] is somewhat middling, but as the small blind raiser, a continuation bet is the standard play to represent strength and potentially take down the pot immediately. While I only have Ace-high with no immediate draw, maintaining aggression is important to keep my ranges balanced and apply pressure to the Big Blind who just called pre-flop.

With Kh 8h on a 6s Jh 5h board, I have a strong King-high flush draw. The pot odds are favorable to call a half-pot continuation bet from the Small Blind. Calling allows me to play in position, keeps my opponent's bluffing range wide, and controls the size of the pot while I try to hit my flush.

The board has become quite coordinated with the turn bringing a third heart, which creates many potential flush draws and straight draws for my opponent. Holding Ace-high with no heart in my hand, I have almost no equity against a calling range that likely hit a pair or a draw. Betting here would be a reckless bluff without substantial fold equity. Checking is the most prudent play to control the pot size, re-evaluate on the river, or potentially take a free card to showdown.

I've hit the second nut flush on the turn after my opponent checked. My hand is extremely strong, and I want to start building the pot to extract value from a lower flush, an overpair, top pair, or hands with a lone high heart redraw like the Ace of hearts. Betting around half pot is a solid sizing to get called by weaker made hands while charging potential draws.

I am facing a bet of 600 into an 1800 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. I hold the Ace of Diamonds, giving me the nut flush draw on a board with two hearts. While my Ace-high is currently behind, my equity with the nut flush draw is significant enough to justify the call, especially since I have implied odds if I hit my diamond on the river. Raising would be risky against an opponent showing strength on the turn, so calling to see the river is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

The board is 6s-Jh-5h-3h-Tc, and I hold Ad-9d (Ace-high). My opponent showed aggression on the turn by betting, suggesting they have at least a pair or a strong draw. With no pair, my hand lacks showdown value, and betting would likely only be called by better hands. I hold the second nut flush on the river after the board completes with a non-heart, and my opponent checks to me again. My hand is extremely strong, losing only to the Ace-high flush. Betting around two-thirds of the pot will extract maximum value from weaker flushes (like Queen-high or Jack-high flushes), sets, or stubborn pairs that might look me up, while not being so large that it folds out everything but the nuts.

My Ace-high hand (Ad, 9d) has no showdown value against an opponent who has shown consistent aggression by betting both the turn and the river on this coordinated board. The board texture [6s, Jh, 5h, 3h, Tc] provides many opportunities for made hands (pairs, sets, straights, or flushes) that beat my Ace-high. Calling a 1500 bet into a 2400 pot without at least a pair is mathematically unfavorable and assumes the opponent is bluffing with total air, which is not supported by their previous betting sequence. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for future hands.

I am holding Ten-High, Five-Low offsuit (Th, 5s) on the button. This is a very weak starting hand with little potential for value. Given that I have 13,700 chips, there is no need to play speculative junk hands out of position or even on the button when the cards are this poor. With Jack-Nine offsuit in the Small Blind, the hand is quite marginal. J8s is a standard open from the Button. I have position on both the SB and BB, and opening to 300 chips (3 big blinds) is consistent with my previous raises, putting pressure on the blinds while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Defending the big blind with Q6s suited is a standard play against a button open in this tournament structure. The pot odds of 200 to win 450 make it a profitable call to see a flop, allowing me to potentially capitalize if I hit a flush draw or pair.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high and a backdoor flush draw on a board of 3d-Ts-Jc. Checking is the standard play here as I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. I have very little equity to bet for value, and a check allows me to see how the opponent proceeds without committing further chips to a marginal hand.

I have top pair on a somewhat wet, albeit rainbow, board (J T 3). Betting for value and protection against overcards and straight draws. A half-pot sized bet of 300 gets value from weaker jacks, middle pairs like tens, and draws while keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-high (Qs 6s) on a board of 3d-Ts-Jc. The opponent (Pro) has bet 300 into a pot of 650. I am currently the short stack with 3,400 chips (34 big blinds). Given my stack size, I want to prioritize hands with better post-flop playability or higher raw equity. Folding here preserves my stack for a more advantageous spot.

I am in the small blind with A3 suited, which is a strong enough holding to play heads-up against the big blind. The button has folded, so I have a good opportunity to see a flop. I will call to complete the big blind and proceed to the flop with a speculative but potentially strong hand.

With 6d 5h offsuit in the big blind facing a small blind limp, checking is the standard and most optimal play. Seeing a free flop in position allows us to realize our equity without risking additional chips.

I am out of position on a paired Queen-high board. My Ace-high hand is not strong enough to bet for value against a potential Queen or a Six in the big blind's range. Checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on later streets.

I flopped trip sixes on a Q-6-6 board, giving me an extremely strong hand. Betting half-pot here starts to build the pot for value while keeping his range as wide as possible, extracting value from weaker holdings that might fold to a larger sizing.

I am in the small blind with Ace-high on a Queen-high board. The opponent, Pro, checked their option pre-flop and then made a small probe bet of 100 into a 100 pot on the flop. Given the pot odds of 3:1, I am getting a good price to call with my two overcards to the sixes and backdoor flush potential, despite being out of position. Folding now would be overly conservative against a potentially weak or marginal betting range.

The board is paired (6s, 6h), and I have Ace-high with a weak backdoor flush draw. There is no reason to lead out with a bet here, as I have very little fold equity and no value to extract. Checking is the most sound strategic move, allowing me to potentially reach a cheap showdown or re-evaluate on the river if the opponent decides to bet.

I have trip sixes on a very dry board, and the turn 2c changes nothing. Flashlight's call on the flop suggests he has some piece of the board, likely a Queen, a medium pocket pair, or a stubborn high card. I want to continue extracting value and building the pot. A half-pot bet is a solid sizing to keep his calling range wide while setting up a decent-sized river bet.

The opponent (Pro) has placed a small bet of 200 into a pot of 600, offering 3:1 pot odds. With Ace-high on a board of Qd-6s-6h-2c, I am essentially playing a bluff-catcher. While I am not beating any pair, the board is very dry and the opponent's betting line (checking pre-flop, then small betting) is consistent with a weak hand or a probe. Given the pot odds, calling is mathematically justified to see the river, where I can re-evaluate if the opponent continues to bet or checks.

The board has paired on the river with the Queen of Hearts, creating a final board of Qd-6s-6h-2c-Qh. My holding of Ace-high is very weak, effectively only beating pure air or missed draws. Given the opponent has shown consistent aggression with small bets on the flop and turn, they likely have at least a pair of Queens or potentially a Six, both of which have me significantly beat. Betting would be a pure bluff with little fold equity, as any pair will call. Checking is the most sound strategic choice to reach showdown and minimize losses against a range that likely has me crushed.

The river brings a second Queen, putting two Queens and two Sixes on the board. My hand is now Sixes full of Queens (6-6-6-Q-Q). If I bet, worse hands like pocket pairs are unlikely to call, all other 6x hands will chop with me, and Qx will call or raise and beat me. Checking behind is the safest and most optimal play to reach showdown.

4d 3s is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially when facing no raise. As I am the button, I have the advantage of position, but this hand is not strong enough to warrant an open or a call. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable strategy in this situation to maintain my stack.

Ten-Three suited is a very weak hand, even when playing from the small blind against the big blind. Given that Flash is relatively short-stacked (33 BBs) and might look for spots to jam or defend aggressively, there is no need to bloat the pot or waste chips stealing with such a marginal holding. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.

With pocket threes on the button in a 3-handed game, I have a clear open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. Raising to my standard 3x (300) size puts pressure on the blinds while keeping the sizing consistent with my other opens. It also gives me the chance to flop a well-hidden set.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a strong hand in three-handed play. The Button (Pro) has opened for 300, which is their standard opening size. Calling allows me to see a flop while keeping the pot size manageable and under control, especially against a large stack that has been active.

I am in the big blind with Qh 6h. The pot has already been opened to 300 by the button and called by the small blind. The pot size is 700, and it only costs me 200 more to call. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am holding suited cards with decent playability, calling is a standard and profitable decision to see a flop in a multi-way pot.

I have missed the flop completely with my King-Queen offsuit. The board of Ace-high (5h-As-8c) is very likely to have hit either the pre-flop raiser (Pro) or the Big Blind (Flashlight). In a three-way pot, leading out into an Ace-high board without any piece of it is a low-percentage play. Checking is the standard and safest option here to see how the other two players react to this board.

I am in the big blind with Queen-high and no pair on an Ace-high board. Checking is the only sensible move here as I have no showdown value or significant equity to bet into the button aggressor. Controlling the pot size is the priority given my lack of a made hand.

With pocket threes on an Ace-high flop in a 3-way pot, betting as a bluff is risky because either opponent could easily hold an Ace or an Eight that they are checking to the pre-flop raiser. Checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity by seeing a free turn card, and potentially hit a two-outer for a set without risking any more chips.

However, in a three-way pot, someone could easily have a medium-strength hand like a Ten, an Eight, or a weak Ace that they are happy to check down. Since I have King-high and a gutshot, checking is the most prudent move. It allows me to potentially see a free river card and realize my equity without risking more of my relatively short stack on a bluff that might not work against two opponents.

On the turn, the board is 5h As 8c Td. I hold Qh 6h, giving me a flush draw. With Flash already checking, the action is on me. The button (Pro) checked the flop, suggesting they may be weak or also playing cautiously. Betting into a multi-way pot on this board when I lack a made hand could be risky, especially since I am out of position. Checking allows me to see the river for free and control the pot size while realizing my equity. If the button checks behind again, I gain a free card; if they bet, I can re-evaluate my equity and pot odds to decide whether to continue.

With pocket threes on a 5-A-8-T board, my hand is very weak but still retains marginal showdown value. Both opponents have checked twice, indicating they likely don't have an Ace, but they could easily have paired the Ten, Eight, or Five, or hold a draw. Betting as a bluff here in a multi-way pot is risky because I will often get called by better pairs, and I don't block any strong hands. Checking behind is the safest option, allowing me to see a free river card and potentially hit my two-outer, while controlling the pot size.

On the river, the Jack of Diamonds completes my Broadway straight (A-K-Q-J-10). By betting 600 into the 900 pot, I am sizing it to look like a potential bluff or a thin value bet with a marginal Ace, hoping to get called by any Ace, two-pair, or even a sticky Jack or Ten.

I hold Queen-high with no pair on an Ace-high board. The small blind, Flash, has bet 600 into a 1500 pot after checking the previous streets. This action strongly suggests a made hand, such as an Ace, a pair of Tens, or a pair of Jacks, all of which beat my Queen-high. With no showdown value and a high probability of being beaten, calling is not a profitable decision. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

On a board of 5-A-8-T-J, my pocket threes are beaten by almost everything. Any pair on the board or even some high-card bluffs beat my hand. Since Flash is leading out for a substantial bet on the river after the action checked down, I have virtually zero showdown value and no compelling reason to bluff raise. Folding is the only logical and profitable choice.

I am on the button with Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. I have 40.5 big blinds. My goal is to extract value and build the pot. A standard 3x open is effective here to narrow the field or take the blinds down immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. The Button (Flash) opened to 300. I'm avoiding a 3-bet as JTo is not a premium hand, and I don't want to bloat the pot out of position against a range that opens from the button.

3c 5h is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand. Facing a raise from the button and a call from the small blind, there is zero reason to defend this hand, even with decent pot odds. Folding is the only sensible choice to preserve chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-high on a board of Queen-Queen-Eight. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. I hold Ace-King high on a paired board of Queen-Queen-Eight. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have the range advantage on this texture, as I am more likely to hold a Queen or high pocket pairs. Flashlight checked to me, which is standard. A continuation bet here serves to fold out lower high-card hands that have equity against me and to extract value from draws or smaller pairs that might stick around for one street. The flop is Q-Q-8, a very dry board that strongly favors the pre-flop aggressor (the button). My hand, Jack-Ten high, has no equity—no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw. Investing more chips here against an opponent who has already shown strength by opening pre-flop and betting the flop is mathematically incorrect and strategically unsound. I am holding 3c 7d on the button. This is a very weak hand that lacks potential to win a showdown or play well post-flop. In a three-handed game, while position is valuable, playing this range is unprofitable. The standard strategy here is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With Pocket Aces in the Small Blind after the Button folds, raising is the best play to build the pot for maximum value. A standard raise to 3x (300) will likely get called by the Big Blind's defending range and disguises the strength of my hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Q2s. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Calling costs 200 more into a pot that will be 600. However, I will choose to fold this time to avoid playing a weak hand out of position against a big stack.

AKo is a premium starting hand. As the first to act on the Button, I want to raise to build the pot and try to isolate an opponent or take down the blinds immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and optimal for this hand.

Ace-Seven offsuit is a relatively strong hand in a three-handed game. The button's 3x raise is a standard opening, and while being out of position in the Small Blind is not ideal, folding A7o here would be too tight. I will call to see a flop and manage my stack accordingly, as I am currently the shortest stack at the table.

I hold 4c-5d, which is a very weak starting hand. Playing trash cards out of position is a recipe for losing chips, so the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I have hit bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that contains two overcards (King and Ten). These cards are very much in the opening range of a button raiser like Pro. Being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand like bottom pair.

With top pair, top kicker (AK) on a rainbow board, my hand is very strong against Flash's likely pre-flop calling range. However, the board has some connectivity, making straight draws like QJ, J9, and 89 possible. Betting here allows me to extract value from worse Kings (like KQ or KJ) and middle pairs, while charging draws to continue. I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board with two overcards. The opponent's bet of 300 into a 700 pot provides good odds for a call, especially since my hand can still beat various semi-bluffs like QJ, J9, or smaller pairs. While out of position, I have enough equity to see a turn card, though I must remain cautious if the action continues to escalate on later streets.

On the turn, the 9 of clubs makes the board even more connected, potentially completing straights like QJ or adding flush draws. I will check to the pre-flop and flop aggressor to see how they react to this turn card. The 9c on the turn is a very connected card. It completes straight draws like QJ and J8, introduces a club flush draw, and pairs up potential flop floats like T9 or 97 to make two pair. While I have top pair, top kicker, my hand is somewhat vulnerable on this wet board. The turn gives me a King-high straight, but the board is very wet with three clubs and a potential higher straight available (AT). Raising here likely only gets called by hands that beat me (higher flushes) and might force out hands I'm beating (two pair, straights without a high club). By calling, I control the size of the pot, keep Pro's range wide, and evaluate the river action based on the final card.

The river 5s is a complete blank, changing nothing from the turn. My King-high straight is very strong, but the board is somewhat scary with three clubs, and AT makes a higher straight. If I bet here, it might be difficult to get called by worse hands like two pair or a single King, as they will be cautious facing three barrels. By checking, I keep the pot controlled, allow my opponent to bluff with any missed straight or backdoor draws, and I can comfortably call a reasonably sized bet.

On the river, the board is Jc 3c Kh Qc 5s. I hold 8c 5c, which gives me an 8-high flush. This check could indicate a marginal hand that is afraid of the flush, or it could be a trap with a higher flush or a full house. Since the pot is already substantial (2400 chips) and my flush is relatively low, betting for value is risky; I am unlikely to get called by many worse hands, and I would be in a difficult spot if raised. Checking behind allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against non-flush hands without risking more chips against a potential higher flush.
